enrichment.seo_title: flowctl Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: flowctl is a free, open-source, single-binary workflow automation tool with approvals, OIDC SSO, RBAC, and remote execution. Features, pricing, alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
flowctl is a free and open-source workflow automation platform built to give engineering and operations teams secure, self-service access to complex, potentially sensitive workflows without requiring day-to-day involvement from a platform or security team. It ships as a single compiled binary, written primarily in Go for the backend with a Svelte and TypeScript frontend, and is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license. The project's central premise, stated directly on its own homepage at flowctl.net, is to let organizations define workflows, such as provisioning infrastructure, granting temporary SSH access to a production instance, running database maintenance jobs, or automating internal business processes, as versioned configuration files, then expose those workflows to authorized users through a web interface with built-in approvals, role-based access control, and audit logging. In its own Show HN launch post, flowctl is described as a modern, lightweight alternative to Rundeck, positioning it against the long-established Java-based job-scheduling and operations-automation tool that has served a similar niche since the mid-2000s.

flowctl's public GitHub repository, hosted at github.com/cvhariharan/flowctl, was created on October 30, 2025, making it a very young project as of mid-2026. It is authored and maintained by a developer using the handle cvhariharan, who identifies himself in a related FOSS United Bengaluru meetup proposal as Hariharan, a software engineer who works on DevOps and infrastructure, with a stated day job as a DevOps engineer at Zerodha, a large Indian financial services and stock-broking firm. There is no indication anywhere in the project's public materials, its website, or its GitHub repository that flowctl is an official Zerodha product or that it has any corporate backing, seed funding, or a formal company structure behind it; it reads as an independent, personal open-source project maintained by a single primary author with community contributions accepted through GitHub issues and pull requests. Hariharan submitted a beginner-level talk proposal introducing flowctl's architecture to the FOSS United Bengaluru chapter for its December 2025 meetup, though public records show the talk was not accepted for that session. Despite its youth, the project has moved quickly: by July 2026 it had accumulated 44 tagged releases, with the latest, v0.13.0, published on June 26, 2026, alongside 325 GitHub stars, 22 forks, and an active commit history, including commits as recent as the same week this profile was compiled, indicating an actively and rapidly iterated codebase rather than an abandoned experiment.

Architecturally, flowctl bundles nearly everything it needs into one binary: a web server that serves both the API and the Svelte-based frontend, a pool of background workers that execute workflow steps, and an internal scheduler that fires cron-based triggers. The only required external dependency is a PostgreSQL database, which stores workflow definitions, execution history, user and team data, and encrypted secrets. Installation follows a short sequence: download a prebuilt binary from GitHub releases (or use the provided Docker Compose file), run flowctl --new-config to scaffold a config.toml file, run flowctl install to apply database migrations, and then flowctl start to bring up the web interface, which listens on localhost:7000 by default. The config.toml file governs a wide surface of operational concerns, including admin bootstrap credentials, the directory where flow definitions live, the network listen address, optional TLS termination, upload size limits, PostgreSQL connection parameters (including full DSN strings or discrete SSL options), a keystore section that manages the encryption keys used to protect stored secrets such as SSH keys and passwords, scheduler tuning parameters like worker count and cron sync interval, SMTP settings for email notifications, webhook notifications that are signed using Ed25519 keys for integrity verification, OIDC settings for single sign-on with support for auto-provisioning users and restricting sign-ups to specific email domains, and a Prometheus-compatible metrics endpoint for observability.

Individual workflows themselves are defined declaratively as files, and flowctl notably supports two config formats: standard YAML, and HUML (Human-oriented Markup Language), a very new, strict, whitespace-sensitive serialization format launched in 2025 specifically to fix YAML's well-known pitfalls around ambiguous indentation and implicit type coercion. flowctl's adoption of HUML as an alternative to YAML is an unusually early one, reflecting its author's evident interest in fast-moving developer-tooling standards; a workflow file specifies a set of inputs the end user must supply, a sequence of actions to run, and where relevant, one or more approval gates that pause execution until a designated approver signs off, plus optional cron schedules that trigger the workflow automatically rather than on demand. Actions can be executed locally on the flowctl host or dispatched over SSH to remote nodes, allowing a single workflow to, for example, provision a cloud resource and then configure it on a fleet of remote machines concurrently. The platform captures the file-based outputs (artifacts) of each action so that later steps in the same workflow can consume them, streams logs from running actions to the web UI in real time so operators can watch a job execute, and automatically retries actions that fail according to configurable retry policies, which together give it the operational feel of a lightweight CI/CD-style execution engine repurposed for self-service operational and business workflows rather than for building and testing software artifacts.

flowctl has no pricing tiers, no paid edition, and no hosted SaaS offering; there is no pricing page on flowctl.net, and the entire codebase is published under the permissive Apache 2.0 license, which allows free commercial and non-commercial use, modification, and self-hosted redistribution. The project does operate a public live demo at demo.flowctl.net so prospective users can try the web interface before deploying it themselves, but production usage requires self-hosting the binary or its Docker image alongside a PostgreSQL instance that the adopting team must provision and operate on its own infrastructure. Because there is no managed cloud version, all operating costs, such as compute for the binary, the PostgreSQL database, TLS certificates, and backups, are borne directly by whoever deploys it, which is typical of small, single-maintainer open-source infrastructure tools at this stage of maturity.

The tool is aimed squarely at platform engineering, DevOps, and site reliability teams inside organizations that already run enough sensitive or repetitive operational processes, such as granting break-glass SSH access, rotating credentials, running database migrations, or provisioning cloud resources, that they want those processes wrapped in an approval workflow and audit trail rather than left to ad hoc scripts or tribal knowledge distributed across Slack channels and internal wikis. Its single-binary, low-dependency design also targets small and mid-sized engineering organizations that want the self-service and governance benefits of larger internal-developer-platform tooling without operating a heavyweight, multi-service system like Airflow, Temporal, or a full Kubernetes-based platform, and without paying for a commercial orchestration or access-management product.

flowctl's most direct point of comparison, by its own author's framing, is Rundeck, a mature, JVM-based, self-service job-runner and operations automation platform that has offered a broadly similar feature set, ad hoc and scheduled job execution, role-based access, approval workflows, and a self-service catalog, since the 2010s, but that carries a heavier JVM runtime footprint and a more complex deployment and licensing model (Rundeck has both an open-source community edition and a commercial PagerDuty-owned enterprise edition). Other adjacent tools include Ansible AWX/Tower, which focuses on running Ansible playbooks with RBAC and scheduling but lacks flowctl's built-in approval-gate concept and is oriented around configuration management rather than general workflow definitions; GitHub Actions and other CI/CD runners, which can be repurposed for operational automation via workflow_dispatch triggers but were not designed for fine-grained internal self-service access control or SSH-based remote execution to arbitrary infrastructure; and general-purpose workflow orchestrators such as Apache Airflow and Temporal, which are powerful but are aimed primarily at data-pipeline and application-workflow orchestration rather than human-in-the-loop operational self-service, and which require considerably more infrastructure to run than flowctl's single binary plus PostgreSQL. flowctl differentiates itself from all of these by combining a minimal deployment footprint, native OIDC SSO and RBAC, encrypted secret storage, and approval gates in one small, opinionated tool rather than requiring teams to assemble a stack of separate access-management, secrets, and job-scheduling systems.

As a project less than a year old at the time of this writing, flowctl is best characterized as an early-stage, actively developed open-source infrastructure tool rather than an established or widely adopted platform; its GitHub metrics, roughly 325 stars, 22 forks, and a handful of open issues and pull requests as of mid-2026, reflect a small but growing community typical of a solo-maintainer project that has recently been surfaced to a wider audience through channels like Hacker News's Show HN and local open-source meetups. The pace of releases, 44 tagged versions in under nine months, suggests the author is iterating quickly on core functionality and stability rather than treating the project as feature-complete, and the presence of Prometheus metrics, OIDC support, and Ed25519-signed webhooks in the configuration surface indicates an ambition toward production-readiness and enterprise-style integration despite the project's small footprint. Prospective adopters should weigh flowctl's low operational overhead and permissive licensing against the risks inherent in relying on a young, single-maintainer open-source project for security-sensitive workflows, including the relative immaturity of its plugin ecosystem, its reliance on GitHub issues as the primary support channel, and the absence of the long production track record that established alternatives like Rundeck or Ansible AWX can offer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: flowctl is a free, open-source, self-hosted workflow automation platform packaged as a single binary. It lets engineering and operations teams define workflows, such as granting temporary SSH access, provisioning infrastructure, running database maintenance, or automating internal business processes, as version-controlled YAML or HUML configuration files, then expose them to authorized users through a web interface with approvals, role-based access control, and audit logging.

The project's public GitHub repository was created on October 30, 2025, and is authored and maintained by a developer known as cvhariharan (Hariharan), a software engineer who works on DevOps and infrastructure. It is presented as an independent open-source project rather than a commercially backed product, and it is written in Go on the backend with a Svelte and TypeScript frontend, licensed under Apache 2.0.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Workflows in flowctl are defined declaratively with inputs, a sequence of actions, and optional approval gates that pause execution until a designated approver signs off. Actions can run locally or be dispatched over SSH to multiple remote nodes concurrently, and workflows can be triggered on demand or automatically via one or more cron schedules.

Security and governance are built in rather than bolted on: authentication integrates with OIDC identity providers for single sign-on, RBAC and team or project namespace isolation control who can view, run, or approve each workflow, and an internal keystore encrypts secrets such as SSH keys and passwords at rest. The web UI streams execution logs in real time, retains execution history, passes file-based artifacts between workflow steps, and can automatically retry failed actions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: flowctl has no pricing tiers, paid edition, or hosted SaaS offering. The entire codebase is published under the Apache 2.0 license, allowing free commercial and non-commercial use, modification, and self-hosted redistribution, and there is no pricing page on flowctl.net.

A public live demo is available at demo.flowctl.net so prospective users can try the web interface before deploying it. Production use requires self-hosting the binary (or its Docker image) alongside a PostgreSQL database that the adopting team provisions and operates itself, so the only real costs are the infrastructure used to run flowctl, not the software.

features.title: Workflow Definition in YAML or HUML
features.description: Workflows are defined as version-controlled configuration files using either standard YAML or HUML (Human-oriented Markup Language), a newer strict configuration format, letting teams specify inputs, actions, and approval gates as code.

features.title: Approval Gates
features.description: Sensitive workflow steps can require sign-off from a designated approver before execution continues, giving teams a built-in change-control mechanism for high-risk operations like production access or infrastructure changes.

features.title: Remote Execution over SSH
features.description: Workflow actions can run locally on the flowctl server or be dispatched concurrently across multiple remote nodes over SSH, enabling fleet-wide operations from a single workflow run.

features.title: Cron-Based Scheduling
features.description: Workflows support multiple cron schedules so recurring operations, such as database maintenance or credential rotation, can run automatically without manual triggering.

features.title: OIDC SSO and Role-Based Access Control
features.description: Authentication integrates with any OIDC-compliant identity provider, with support for automatic user provisioning and domain-restricted sign-ups, while RBAC and team or project namespace isolation control who can view, run, or approve each workflow.

features.title: Encrypted Secrets Storage
features.description: A built-in keystore encrypts sensitive values such as SSH keys and passwords at rest, so workflows can reference credentials without exposing them in plain text configuration files.

features.title: Real-Time Streaming Logs and Execution History
features.description: The web interface streams logs from running actions live and retains a searchable history of past executions, including status and results, for auditability.

features.title: Artifacts and Retry Handling
features.description: Actions can produce file-based artifacts that later steps in the same workflow consume, and failed actions can be automatically retried according to configurable retry policies.

pros: Free and fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no licensing cost or paid tier
Single binary with only PostgreSQL as an external dependency, making self-hosting simple compared to multi-service alternatives
Combines workflow automation with access-control features (approvals, RBAC, OIDC SSO, encrypted secrets) that many teams otherwise assemble from separate tools
Actively developed, with 44 releases published within roughly nine months and ongoing commits
cons: Very young project (created October 2025) with a small community (roughly 325 GitHub stars and 22 forks) and a limited plugin and integration ecosystem compared to mature tools like Rundeck or Ansible AWX
No official Windows binaries; only Linux and macOS builds are published in releases
Requires self-hosting a PostgreSQL instance and the binary yourself; there is no managed cloud or SaaS offering
Support is limited mainly to GitHub issues, and the project relies heavily on a single primary maintainer

faqs.question: What is flowctl?
faqs.answer: flowctl is a free, open-source, single-binary workflow automation platform that lets teams define operational workflows, such as granting temporary SSH access, provisioning infrastructure, or running maintenance jobs, as YAML or HUML configuration files, then expose them through a self-hosted web interface with built-in approvals, scheduling, and role-based access control.

faqs.question: Is flowctl free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. flowctl is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license and is entirely free to download, self-host, and modify. There is no paid tier, subscription, or hosted SaaS version; the only cost is the infrastructure a team uses to run the binary and its required PostgreSQL database.

faqs.question: Who built flowctl?
faqs.answer: flowctl is developed and maintained on GitHub under the handle cvhariharan by Hariharan, a software engineer who works on DevOps and infrastructure. It is presented as an independent open-source project rather than a product of a specific company.

faqs.question: What are the system requirements to run flowctl?
faqs.answer: flowctl runs as a single compiled binary (or via the provided Docker Compose setup) and requires only a PostgreSQL database as an external dependency. Official binaries are published for Linux (x86_64, arm64, and armv7) and macOS (x86_64 and arm64); no official Windows build is provided.

faqs.question: How does flowctl compare to Rundeck or Ansible AWX?
faqs.answer: flowctl positions itself as a lighter-weight alternative to Rundeck, offering similar self-service job execution, approvals, and RBAC without a JVM runtime or commercial enterprise edition. Compared with Ansible AWX, flowctl is not tied to Ansible playbooks and treats approval gates and OIDC SSO as first-class concepts rather than add-ons.

faqs.question: Does flowctl support single sign-on?
faqs.answer: Yes. flowctl integrates with OIDC-compliant identity providers for SSO, and its configuration supports automatic user creation on first login as well as restricting sign-ups to approved email domains.

use_cases.title: Self-service infrastructure access
use_cases.description: Platform teams define workflows that grant time-boxed SSH access or provision cloud resources on request, with an approval gate ensuring a human signs off before access is granted, reducing standing access and manual ticket handling.

use_cases.title: Operational runbooks with approvals
use_cases.description: SRE and operations teams codify sensitive runbooks, such as database maintenance, credential rotation, or production configuration changes, as flowctl workflows so they run consistently, get logged, and require sign-off before execution.

use_cases.title: Internal business process automation
use_cases.description: Because flowctl's executor architecture is domain-agnostic, teams outside of infrastructure can use it to automate custom internal business processes, exposing them as self-service forms with cron scheduling and access control instead of building bespoke internal tools.
use_cases.audience: Internal tooling and backend engineering teams

features: Single-binary or Docker Compose self-hosted deployment
OIDC SSO, RBAC, approvals, and encrypted secrets included
Community support via GitHub issues and pull requests

enrichment.short_description: FlowForge is the commercial platform built by the original Node-RED maintainers that adds enterprise hosting, DevOps Pipelines, and edge device management on top of the open-source Node-RED flow-based programming tool. The company has since rebranded its product and website as FlowFuse.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (open-source self-hosted core plus paid Cloud and Enterprise tiers)
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted open source), or $20/month for hosted Cloud Starter
enrichment.seo_title: FlowForge Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: FlowForge (now FlowFuse) reviewed: Node-RED hosting, DevOps Pipelines, IIoT device management, pricing tiers from free self-hosted to Enterprise, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
FlowForge is the company that turned Node-RED, the open-source flow-based visual programming tool for wiring together hardware devices, APIs, and online services, into a managed enterprise platform. Node-RED itself lets developers drag and drop nodes representing devices, APIs, and logic blocks onto a browser-based canvas and wire them together into 'flows' that run on a lightweight Node.js runtime, making it popular for Internet of Things prototyping, home automation, and industrial data integration. FlowForge does not replace Node-RED; it wraps it with the infrastructure, governance, and collaboration features that individual open-source instances lack, including centralized hosting, team access control, version history, and fleets of remotely managed edge devices. In August 2023 the company rebranded its product, website, and public identity from FlowForge to FlowFuse following a trademark dispute over the FlowForge name, while keeping the same team, technology, and mission; flowforge.com now redirects to flowfuse.com, and the underlying GitHub organization, npm packages, and Docker images were migrated from the flowforge namespace to FlowFuse.

The history of the underlying technology predates the company by nearly a decade. Node-RED was created by Nick O'Leary while he worked in IBM's Emerging Technology Group; he built the first rough prototype over a single weekend in January 2013, and his IBM colleague Dave Conway-Jones quickly joined to help refine it. The first Node-RED code was pushed to GitHub on September 5, 2013, as a quiet soft launch, and the project grew into one of the most widely used low-code tools in the industrial IoT and home-automation communities. Node-RED became one of the founding projects of the JS Foundation in October 2016, and that foundation later merged into the OpenJS Foundation, which continues to host the open-source project today; FlowForge itself joined the OpenJS Foundation as a member organization in December 2022, formalizing its relationship with the community project it commercializes.

FlowForge Inc. was founded in 2021 by Nick O'Leary, who serves as the company's Chief Technology Officer and remains a lead maintainer of the open-source Node-RED project, giving the company a direct line into the roadmap of the technology it builds on. In January 2022, Zeger-Jan van de Weg, an early employee at GitLab, joined as Chief Executive Officer to lead the commercial side of the business. The company is headquartered in San Francisco, California, but operates as a largely remote team drawing talent from Europe, North America, and Asia, and public estimates put headcount in the range of roughly 25 to 30 employees. FlowForge's early funding came from GitLab co-founder and CEO Sid Sijbrandij, who invested through his Open Core Ventures vehicle, a firm dedicated to backing open-core software companies. The company then raised a 7.2 million dollar seed round announced in November 2022, led by a group that included Cota Capital, Westwave Capital, Uncorrelated Ventures, and Open Core Ventures, bringing total disclosed funding to roughly 14.5 million dollars. That capital has funded engineering, product, customer success, marketing, sales, and community roles as the company built out its production-grade hosting and device-management infrastructure.

Technically, the FlowForge or FlowFuse platform sits as a management layer above one or more Node-RED runtime instances. Each Node-RED instance is still the familiar browser-based flow editor where developers wire together nodes for protocols, hardware integrations, databases, and cloud services, backed by a large ecosystem of community-contributed nodes distributed through npm. What FlowForge adds is centralized instance management so teams can spin up, monitor, and retire many Node-RED instances from one dashboard; a Device Agent that allows a single instance's logic to be deployed and remotely monitored across hundreds or thousands of edge devices on factory floors or in the field; DevOps Pipelines that stage a flow through development, test, and production environments with promotion and rollback controls; snapshot-based version history so changes can be reviewed and reverted; role-based access control and audit logging for team collaboration; and enterprise security features such as TLS encryption and SAML-based single sign-on. A built-in MQTT broker supports lightweight machine-to-machine messaging common in industrial and IoT deployments, and 'Project Nodes' allow secure message exchange between separate FlowForge instances for edge-to-cloud data flows. The platform is offered both as a fully managed cloud service and as a self-hosted deployment that organizations can run inside their own infrastructure, including air-gapped environments where cloud connectivity is restricted for security or compliance reasons.

Pricing is split between Cloud-hosted and Self-Hosted tracks, each with Starter, Pro (sometimes called Team), and Enterprise tiers. On the Cloud track, Starter costs 20 dollars per month after an initial Pro-tier free trial, includes one hosted Node-RED instance (upgradable to three hosted or three remote instances) and up to two team members, and bundles the FlowFuse Expert AI assistant and pre-built Blueprint templates. The Cloud Pro tier costs 425 dollars per month, or a discounted rate with one free month when billed annually, and steps up to five hosted or remote instances, twenty team members, an eight-client MQTT broker, and standard support backed by a 99.5 percent uptime service level agreement. Cloud Enterprise is quote-based, scales to twenty or more instances with unlimited team members, and adds single sign-on, a twenty-client MQTT broker, device group management, and enterprise support with a 99.9 percent SLA. The Self-Hosted track mirrors this structure: a free, fully open-source Starter tier supports up to five instances and five team members for evaluation or small deployments; a Self-Hosted Pro tier costs 425 dollars per month billed annually and adds installation support and a dedicated customer success contact on top of the same instance and seat limits as Cloud Pro; and a Self-Hosted Enterprise tier is available by quote or trial license with unlimited team members and twenty-plus instances for organizations that must keep everything on-premises.

The platform's core audience is engineering and operations teams inside manufacturing, industrial automation, and Internet of Things organizations, spanning sectors such as automotive, renewable energy, semiconductors, and aerospace, who need to connect shop-floor equipment, SCADA and MES systems, and enterprise software without writing brittle point-to-point integrations. It also appeals to systems integrators and IoT solution builders who previously ran self-managed, unsupported Node-RED instances and want production-grade hosting, security certifications, and support without abandoning the visual flow-programming model their teams already know. FlowForge holds SOC 2 Type 1 and Type 2 certifications, which matters for enterprise buyers evaluating data-handling practices in regulated or security-conscious environments.

Competitively, FlowForge's most direct alternative is simply self-hosting open-source Node-RED without any commercial layer, which remains free but pushes all hosting, scaling, security, and device-fleet management work onto the customer's own engineering team. In the broader low-code automation and integration space, it competes with workflow tools such as n8n, Zapier, and Make (formerly Integromat), though those products are oriented toward SaaS-to-SaaS business automation rather than industrial hardware and protocol connectivity. Within industrial automation and IIoT specifically, it overlaps with platforms such as Tulip and Ignition from Inductive Automation, and with general-purpose internal-tool builders like Retool for the dashboard and visualization side of its offering. FlowForge's core differentiation is that it is built and maintained by the same engineers who created and continue to steward the open-source Node-RED project itself, giving customers first-party expertise, tight product-roadmap alignment with the underlying runtime, and a credible open-core story rather than a third-party wrapper around someone else's open-source tool.

In terms of product evolution, the company has progressively layered more enterprise and AI capabilities onto its original hosting service, including FlowFuse Expert, an AI-assisted feature that lets users describe an integration or automation need in plain language and receive a generated Node-RED flow as a starting point, and a library of Blueprint templates for common industrial and IoT use cases that reduce the time needed to build a working flow from scratch. The August 2023 rebrand to FlowFuse was purely a naming and trademark change rather than a technical pivot, with the company explicitly stating that the platform, cloud service, and roadmap continued unchanged under the new name, and choosing 'FlowFuse' specifically because it stayed close to the original name, reflected the idea of fusing together data, processes, and technologies across a factory or enterprise, and preserved the 'FF' prefix already used throughout its environment variables and code base.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: FlowForge is the commercial company built around Node-RED, the open-source flow-based low-code tool used to wire together hardware devices, APIs, and online services on a visual, browser-based canvas. Rather than replacing Node-RED, FlowForge adds the hosting, governance, and device-fleet management layer that turns individual open-source Node-RED instances into a production-grade platform for teams and enterprises.

The company was founded in 2021 by Nick O'Leary, the original creator and lead maintainer of Node-RED, who first built the tool at IBM in 2013. In August 2023 the company rebranded from FlowForge to FlowFuse following a trademark dispute; the underlying product, team, and technology stayed the same, and flowforge.com now redirects to flowfuse.com.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform's core additions on top of the Node-RED editor include centralized management of multiple instances, a Device Agent for deploying and monitoring flows across hundreds or thousands of remote edge devices, and DevOps Pipelines that promote a flow through development, test, and production environments with rollback support via version-history snapshots.

Enterprise features cover role-based access control, single sign-on through SAML providers, audit logging, an integrated MQTT broker for machine-to-machine messaging, high-availability scaling with automatic failover, and both cloud-hosted and self-hosted (including air-gapped) deployment options. An AI assistant called FlowFuse Expert can generate starter flows from plain-language descriptions, and a Blueprints library provides pre-built templates for common integration patterns.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pricing splits into Cloud-hosted and Self-Hosted tracks, each with Starter, Pro, and Enterprise tiers. Cloud Starter runs 20 dollars per month after a free Pro trial with one to three instances and two team members; Cloud Pro is 425 dollars per month (discounted annually) with five instances, twenty team members, an MQTT broker, and a 99.5 percent SLA; Cloud Enterprise is quote-based with unlimited seats, SSO, and a 99.9 percent SLA.

The Self-Hosted Starter tier is free and fully open source, supporting up to five instances and five team members for evaluation or small deployments. Self-Hosted Pro costs 425 dollars per month billed annually and adds installation support, while Self-Hosted Enterprise is quote or trial-license based for organizations that need everything running on their own infrastructure.

features.title: Node-RED visual flow editor
features.description: A browser-based, drag-and-drop canvas for wiring together devices, APIs, databases, and services using a large ecosystem of community and certified nodes.

features.title: DevOps Pipelines
features.description: Promote a flow through development, test, and production instances with staged rollouts and one-click promotion between environments.

features.title: Device Agent for edge fleets
features.description: Deploy and remotely monitor Node-RED logic across hundreds or thousands of edge devices in factories, plants, or the field from a single dashboard.

features.title: Version history and snapshots
features.description: Automatic snapshots of instance state let teams review changes over time and roll back to a previous working flow if something breaks.

features.title: Team collaboration and RBAC
features.description: Multiple developers can work against the same instance with role-based access control governing who can view, edit, or deploy flows.

features.title: Enterprise security (SSO and audit logs)
features.description: TLS/SSL encryption, SAML-based single sign-on, and full audit logging of configuration and flow changes for compliance-sensitive teams.

features.title: Built-in MQTT broker
features.description: An included MQTT broker supports lightweight machine-to-machine messaging common in industrial and IoT deployments, with client limits that scale by plan.

features.title: FlowFuse Expert AI assistant and Blueprints
features.description: An AI assistant generates starter flows from plain-language descriptions, and a library of Blueprint templates accelerates common integration and dashboard patterns.

pros: Built and maintained by the original creators and lead maintainers of the open-source Node-RED project, so the roadmap stays tightly aligned with the underlying runtime.
Free, fully open-source self-hosted tier available for evaluation or small teams before committing to a paid plan.
Purpose-built for industrial IoT and edge scenarios, with device-fleet management and staged DevOps Pipelines that generic automation tools do not offer.
SOC 2 Type 1 and Type 2 certifications, plus self-hosted and air-gapped deployment options for regulated enterprises.
cons: The August 2023 rebrand to FlowFuse means the FlowForge name and flowforge.com domain no longer point to an actively marketed brand, which can confuse users searching under the old name.
Paid Cloud and Self-Hosted Pro tiers jump from a low-cost Starter plan to 425 dollars per month, a steep step for small teams outgrowing the free or Starter tier.
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation, making upfront budgeting harder for prospective customers.

Node-RED's low-code flow model still requires developer-level familiarity with JavaScript, protocols, and data structures for anything beyond simple wiring, so it is not a true no-code tool for non-technical business users.

faqs.question: Is FlowForge the same company as FlowFuse?
faqs.answer: Yes. FlowForge Inc. rebranded its product and public identity to FlowFuse in August 2023 after a trademark dispute over the FlowForge name. The team, technology, and product roadmap did not change, and flowforge.com now redirects to flowfuse.com.

faqs.question: What is the relationship between FlowForge and Node-RED?
faqs.answer: Node-RED is the open-source flow-based programming tool created by Nick O'Leary at IBM in 2013. FlowForge is the company Nick O'Leary later founded in 2021 to commercialize Node-RED, adding hosting, device management, DevOps Pipelines, and enterprise security on top of the open-source runtime.

faqs.question: Can I use FlowForge for free?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Self-Hosted Starter tier is free and fully open source, supporting up to five Node-RED instances and five team members, making it suitable for evaluation, hobby projects, or small deployments before upgrading to a paid Cloud or Pro tier.

faqs.question: Who founded FlowForge and when?
faqs.answer: FlowForge was founded in 2021 by Nick O'Leary, the creator and lead maintainer of Node-RED, who serves as CTO. Zeger-Jan van de Weg, an early GitLab employee, joined as CEO in January 2022. The company is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: How much funding has FlowForge raised?
faqs.answer: FlowForge raised an initial 2 million dollars from GitLab CEO Sid Sijbrandij through Open Core Ventures, followed by a 7.2 million dollar seed round in November 2022 led by Cota Capital, Westwave Capital, Uncorrelated Ventures, and Open Core Ventures, bringing total disclosed funding to roughly 14.5 million dollars.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to FlowForge?

enrichment.seo_title: Flowise Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Flowise review 2026: pricing tiers, key features, pros, cons, and top alternatives for this open-source, drag-and-drop builder for LLM apps and AI agents.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Flowise, developed by FlowiseAI, is an open-source, low-code platform that lets developers and technically-minded non-developers build large language model (LLM) applications, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) chatbots, and multi-agent orchestration systems through a visual, drag-and-drop canvas. Rather than writing orchestration code by hand, users assemble pre-built nodes representing LLMs, prompt templates, memory modules, vector stores, document loaders, tools, and agent logic, then wire them together into an executable flow. Under the hood, Flowise draws heavily on LangChain-style abstractions, exposing them as visual components so that connecting a chat model to a vector database or adding a tool-calling agent takes minutes instead of custom Python or TypeScript scripting. Every flow can be deployed as a REST API, embedded as a chat widget, or accessed through official TypeScript and Python SDKs, which makes Flowise usable both as a no-code prototyping tool and as a backend orchestration layer for production applications.

The project was founded in 2023 by software engineers Henry Heng, who serves as chief executive, and Chung Yau Ong. Flowise went through Y Combinator's Summer 2023 batch, and the young company raised early seed capital with participation from Y Combinator, GPU Ventures, and Nvidia's Inception Program for startups, according to Crunchbase. Despite operating with a very small core team, widely reported at only a handful of full-time employees, Flowise grew quickly in the open-source AI tooling space: its GitHub repository, FlowiseAI/Flowise, has accumulated more than 54,000 stars and roughly 24,700 forks, is licensed under Apache 2.0, and has seen more than 3,600 commits and over 80 tagged releases. The codebase is written primarily in TypeScript and JavaScript, pairing a React front-end canvas with a Node.js backend API, and the project maintains active documentation at docs.flowiseai.com plus a large community Discord server. The trajectory of the company culminated in August 2025, when enterprise HR and finance software company Workday announced it had acquired Flowise, with the explicit goal of using Flowise's agent-builder technology to accelerate AI agent creation across the Workday platform for its own customers and partners. The financial terms of the acquisition were not publicly disclosed, and Flowise has continued to operate its open-source project and Flowise Cloud offering under the Workday umbrella.

Technically, Flowise organizes its building blocks into a node-based graph editor. Chatflows handle simpler, single-agent conversational use cases with RAG support pulling from PDFs, spreadsheets, websites, and other data sources, while Agentflows support multi-agent systems in which several specialized agents coordinate on more complex tasks, optionally with human-in-the-loop checkpoints that pause a flow for human review or approval before it continues. Flowise supports well over 100 different LLM providers, embedding models, and vector databases out of the box, including OpenAI, Anthropic, and open-weight models served locally, alongside vector stores such as Pinecone, Weaviate, and Chroma. For teams that need visibility into what their agents are doing in production, Flowise exposes execution traces and metrics compatible with OpenTelemetry and Prometheus, and enterprise deployments add role-based access control, audit logging, and SSO/SAML support. Because the core project is open source under Apache 2.0, it can be self-hosted for free using Docker or deployed to any major cloud provider, including AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, and DigitalOcean, giving organizations full control over data residency and infrastructure costs.

On pricing, Flowise splits into two tracks. The self-hosted, open-source version is entirely free to run, subject only to the infrastructure and any third-party LLM API costs a user incurs. The managed Flowise Cloud track offers a Free plan with two flows, 100 predictions per month, 5 MB of storage, and community support, aimed at individuals testing the product. Above that sits a Starter plan priced at roughly $35 per month with unlimited flows, 10,000 predictions per month, and 1 GB of storage, intended for individual builders and small projects. A Pro plan at roughly $65 per month raises the monthly prediction allowance to 50,000, expands storage to about 10 GB, and adds unlimited workspaces along with user-management controls for small teams. Above Pro, Flowise offers a custom-priced Enterprise tier that layers on single sign-on, SAML, granular role-based permissions, audit logs, dedicated support, and options for on-premises or air-gapped deployment for organizations with strict compliance or data-sovereignty requirements; pricing for this tier is negotiated directly with the Flowise sales team rather than published. Because LLM inference is typically billed separately by the underlying model provider, the subscription price on Flowise Cloud generally does not include token usage costs, and production RAG deployments that require a dedicated vector database add further cost outside the Flowise subscription itself.

Flowise's target users span a wide range of technical sophistication. Individual developers and indie builders use it to prototype chatbots and AI assistants quickly without hand-rolling LangChain code. Small and medium-sized product teams use it to stand up internal tools, customer-support bots, and RAG-powered knowledge assistants that plug into existing data sources. At the enterprise end, the company has cited large organizations, including AWS, Priceline, Accenture, Deloitte, and Publicis Groupe, as users or partners exploring Flowise for building and governing AI agents at scale, which speaks to its positioning as both an accessible low-code tool and an enterprise-grade agent-orchestration platform once Workday's ownership and enterprise distribution muscle are factored in.

Within the crowded landscape of visual AI-agent and LLM-orchestration tools, Flowise is most frequently compared to Langflow, another open-source, LangChain-adjacent drag-and-drop flow builder with a similar node-graph interface; n8n, a broader general-purpose workflow-automation platform that has added AI and LLM nodes on top of its long-standing integration automation core; Dify, an open-source LLMOps platform that combines visual flow building with more opinionated application templates and built-in vector database management; and LangChain and its LangGraph framework, which provide the underlying programmatic abstractions that tools like Flowise wrap in a visual interface, appealing more to engineers comfortable writing code directly. Flowise differentiates itself from these alternatives through its especially low barrier to entry for RAG and multi-agent use cases, its permissive Apache 2.0 license that supports unrestricted self-hosting, its dual TypeScript/Python SDK support, and, since mid-2025, the enterprise credibility and go-to-market backing that comes from being owned by a major public enterprise software vendor in Workday. Where n8n leans toward general business-process automation and Dify leans toward packaged LLM application templates, Flowise keeps its focus tightly on flexible, visually composed agent and chatbot orchestration, making it a common choice for teams that want LangChain-level flexibility without writing orchestration code by hand.

Product evolution since the 2023 launch has moved from a relatively simple LangChain-flow visual wrapper toward a more complete agent-development platform, adding Agentflow multi-agent orchestration, human-in-the-loop review checkpoints, observability integrations, and enterprise security controls as the market shifted from single-turn chatbots toward autonomous, multi-step AI agents. The Workday acquisition marks the most significant milestone in the project's short history, transitioning Flowise from an independent, YC-backed open-source startup into a strategic AI-agent-building component inside a major enterprise software suite, while the underlying open-source repository continues to be maintained and remains free to self-host for developers and organizations that prefer not to use the managed cloud offering.

From a market-position standpoint, Flowise sits at the intersection of two fast-growing categories: no-code and low-code application builders, and the newer wave of agent-orchestration frameworks that emerged alongside LangChain, LlamaIndex, and similar developer tooling. Its early advantage came from timing and openness, launching in 2023 just as demand for RAG chatbots and custom GPT-style assistants exploded, and choosing a permissive Apache 2.0 license at a moment when many competitors, including hosted-only or source-available alternatives, kept their core orchestration engines closed or restrictively licensed. That openness helped Flowise build one of the larger open-source communities among visual AI-agent builders, reflected in its GitHub star count and active Discord, and it is frequently referenced alongside Langflow and Dify in comparison articles and 'best open-source AI agent builder' roundups. The Workday acquisition changes Flowise's competitive calculus going forward: rather than competing purely as an independent open-source project chasing venture funding, Flowise now benefits from an enterprise parent with existing relationships across HR, finance, and operations software at large organizations, which could accelerate enterprise adoption of Flowise-built agents even as the open-source community version continues to be maintained separately. For teams evaluating Flowise today, the practical takeaway is that it remains one of the most accessible ways to move from a LangChain prototype to a deployable, API-exposed AI agent without abandoning the option to self-host, while its Cloud and Enterprise tiers offer a managed path for teams that would rather not operate their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Flowise is an open-source, low-code platform for visually building LLM applications, RAG chatbots, and multi-agent AI systems. Instead of writing orchestration code, users drag and connect nodes representing language models, prompts, memory, tools, vector stores, and agent logic onto a canvas to assemble a working AI flow.

Founded in 2023 by Henry Heng and Chung Yau Ong and backed by Y Combinator, the project grew into one of the most starred open-source AI agent-builder repositories on GitHub before being acquired by Workday in August 2025, which now uses Flowise's technology to power AI agent creation across its own enterprise platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Flowise supports two main flow types: Chatflows for single-agent conversational assistants with RAG support, and Agentflows for coordinated multi-agent systems that can include human-in-the-loop review checkpoints before an action is finalized.

The platform integrates with more than 100 LLM providers, embedding models, and vector databases, exposes flows as REST APIs, embeddable chat widgets, and TypeScript/Python SDKs, and provides observability through OpenTelemetry- and Prometheus-compatible execution traces for production monitoring.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Flowise Works
paragraphs: Flowise's canvas is built on a node-graph editor where each node represents a discrete building block, such as a chat model, a document loader, a vector store retriever, or a custom tool. Users connect these nodes to define how data and control flow through the application, drawing on LangChain-style abstractions under the hood.

The underlying codebase is written primarily in TypeScript and JavaScript, with a React front-end and Node.js backend, and is released under the Apache 2.0 license. This lets organizations self-host Flowise for free on Docker or any major cloud provider, including AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, and DigitalOcean, while retaining full control over data and infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Flowise offers two pricing tracks: a free, self-hosted open-source option, and a managed Flowise Cloud with a Free plan, a Starter plan at roughly $35 per month, and a Pro plan at roughly $65 per month, each raising limits on predictions per month, storage, and workspace or user management.

An Enterprise tier with custom pricing adds SSO/SAML, role-based access control, audit logging, dedicated support, and on-premises or air-gapped deployment options for organizations with strict security or compliance needs. LLM token usage from providers like OpenAI or Anthropic is typically billed separately from the Flowise subscription itself.

long_form_blocks.heading: Competitors and Alternatives
paragraphs: Flowise is most often compared to Langflow, another open-source visual LangChain-style flow builder; n8n, a broader workflow-automation platform that has added AI and LLM nodes; Dify, an open-source LLMOps platform with more opinionated application templates; and LangChain/LangGraph, the underlying code-first frameworks that tools like Flowise wrap in a visual interface.

Flowise differentiates itself with its permissive Apache 2.0 license, dual TypeScript and Python SDK support, tight focus on visual agent and RAG orchestration rather than general business-process automation, and, since its 2025 acquisition, the enterprise distribution and credibility that comes from being owned by Workday.

enrichment.short_description: Fluent Bit is a CNCF graduated, open-source log, metric, and trace processor and forwarder written in C, built for lightweight collection and routing of telemetry data in cloud-native, containerized, and edge environments.
enrichment.seo_title: Fluent Bit Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fluent Bit review 2026: a CNCF graduated, open-source log and metrics forwarder from Treasure Data. See features, architecture, pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Fluent Bit is an open-source telemetry pipeline that collects, parses, filters, buffers, and routes logs, metrics, and traces from a wide range of sources to a wide range of destinations. It is written entirely in C, which gives it an unusually small runtime footprint (the compiled core is roughly 450 kilobytes) and near-zero external dependencies, making it suitable for resource-constrained environments such as embedded Linux devices, IoT gateways, edge nodes, and sidecar or DaemonSet deployments inside Kubernetes clusters. Architecturally it is event-driven and asynchronous, using a chunk-based buffering system that can spill to disk or stay in memory, and a tag-based routing engine that lets a single Fluent Bit process ingest from dozens of input plugins and dispatch to dozens of output plugins simultaneously without duplicating data pipelines.

The project traces its origin to 2014, when Eduardo Silva, an engineer working on the Fluentd team at Treasure Data, identified the need for a lighter-weight sibling to Fluentd that could run inside constrained environments like embedded Linux systems and network gateways where Fluentd's Ruby-based runtime was too heavy. Fluent Bit was written from scratch in C and released under the Apache License 2.0 as a new, independent open-source project that shared Fluentd's philosophy of unifying data collection but targeted a fundamentally different performance envelope. As container adoption accelerated in the mid-to-late 2010s, the cloud-native community pushed Fluent Bit's scope well beyond its original embedded-systems use case, and it became one of the default choices for shipping logs and metrics out of Kubernetes clusters.

Fluent Bit lives inside the Cloud Native Computing Foundation's Fluent project family, the same CNCF-hosted umbrella that includes Fluentd. Fluentd itself joined CNCF as an incubating project in 2016 and reached graduated status in April 2019, and Fluent Bit is recognized alongside it as a CNCF graduated project, the foundation's highest maturity tier, reserved for projects that have demonstrated broad adoption, a documented and diverse governance structure, and sustained production use at scale. Adoption metrics published by the project and CNCF underline that scale: Fluent Bit passed 1 billion cumulative downloads in March 2022, surpassed 13 billion downloads by March 2024 when version 3 shipped, and the official Fluent Bit website now cites more than 15 billion deployments, with the software processing over one petabyte of telemetry data daily across more than a billion monitored data sources worldwide.

Technically, a Fluent Bit configuration is built from four pipeline stages: inputs, which collect data using plugins such as tail (for log files), systemd, docker, kubernetes, forward, syslog, http, and dozens of specialized collectors; parsers, which normalize raw text or binary records into structured key-value events using regex, JSON, or LTSV parsing rules; filters (also called processors in newer releases), which can enrich, drop, mask, or reshape records using plugins like kubernetes (for pod metadata enrichment), grep, record_modifier, and lua for custom scripting logic; and outputs, which deliver the processed records to destinations ranging from Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, and Splunk to cloud-native services like Amazon S3, CloudWatch, Kinesis Firehose, Azure Blob and Log Analytics, Google Cloud Logging and BigQuery, Kafka, InfluxDB, Loki, and Fluentd itself as a downstream aggregator. The project ships more than 100 built-in plugins across these categories. Later major versions modernized the pipeline further: version 2.x introduced a formal processors API and refined buffering and metrics handling, while version 3.x, released in 2024, added native OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP) ingestion and egress, SQL-like stream processing over telemetry data, and support for WebAssembly (WASM) filter and input plugins, positioning Fluent Bit as a first-class OpenTelemetry-compatible edge agent rather than only a classic log shipper.

Fluent Bit's software itself carries no license fee or paid tier; the entire codebase is free and open source under Apache 2.0, and any organization can self-host and operate it without a commercial agreement. A commercial layer around the project has existed since 2020, when Eduardo Silva co-founded Calyptia in San Francisco together with Anurag Gupta, explicitly to build managed and enterprise tooling on top of Fluentd and Fluent Bit, which the two had created and continued to maintain. Calyptia raised a five million dollar seed round in 2022 led by Sierra Ventures and Carbide Ventures and built products such as Calyptia Cloud and Calyptia Core for centrally managing fleets of Fluent Bit agents. On January 22, 2024, observability vendor Chronosphere announced its acquisition of Calyptia, and Chronosphere subsequently became the primary corporate sponsor of the open-source Fluent Bit project, retaining Eduardo Silva to lead a dedicated team of Chronosphere engineers on the project full time. Chronosphere now offers a commercial 'Telemetry Pipeline' product built on Fluent Bit for enterprises that want centralized fleet management, data transformation, and observability cost-control tooling layered on top of the open-source agent; pricing for that commercial product is not publicly listed and requires contacting Chronosphere's sales team, while the underlying Fluent Bit software remains entirely free to download, self-host, and modify.

The primary users of Fluent Bit are platform engineers, site reliability engineers, and DevOps teams operating containerized or cloud-native infrastructure, particularly Kubernetes clusters where Fluent Bit is commonly deployed as a per-node DaemonSet to collect container and node logs before forwarding them to a central store or aggregator. It is also widely embedded by cloud providers as default tooling: Amazon uses Fluent Bit as the log router for ECS and Fargate through FireLens and offers it as a supported add-on for EKS, and it appears as a standard logging option in Google Kubernetes Engine and various managed Kubernetes offerings from other providers. Beyond Kubernetes, its low footprint makes it a natural fit for edge computing, IoT gateways, and embedded Linux devices, which remains close to its original 2014 design goal.

Within the observability tooling landscape, Fluent Bit is most often compared against four alternatives. Fluentd, its own predecessor and sibling CNCF project, offers a much larger plugin ecosystem written in Ruby with C extensions but carries a heavier memory and CPU footprint, which is why many architectures pair the two together, using Fluent Bit as a lightweight forwarder at the edge and Fluentd as a heavier central aggregator. Logstash, part of the Elastic Stack, runs on the JVM and offers powerful data transformation via its Ruby-based filter language, but its Java runtime overhead makes it a poor fit for constrained or high-density deployments compared to Fluent Bit's C-based design. Vector, a Rust-based collector originally built by Timber.io, is frequently benchmarked as delivering higher raw throughput than Fluent Bit under heavy load while offering a similarly small footprint, making it Fluent Bit's closest performance competitor among newer entrants. The OpenTelemetry Collector, the vendor-neutral reference implementation from the OpenTelemetry project, has become an increasingly common default for greenfield deployments that want a single, standards-based pipeline for logs, metrics, and traces; Fluent Bit has responded by deepening its own native OTLP support so it can interoperate with or substitute for the OTel Collector in resource-constrained roles.

Fluent Bit's notable differentiators are its CNCF graduated maturity status, its extremely small and dependency-free binary, its native fit as a Kubernetes DaemonSet log collector, its broad plugin catalog spanning most major cloud and observability backends, and its growing native OpenTelemetry compatibility. Its main limitations relative to alternatives are a steeper learning curve for advanced Lua-based filtering and stream processing compared to some newer tools, and reliance on a single primary corporate sponsor, Chronosphere, for full-time engineering investment, though governance remains under CNCF rather than any single vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Fluent Bit is a free, open-source telemetry pipeline that collects logs, metrics, and traces from a wide variety of sources and forwards them to a wide variety of destinations. Written in C, it is designed for minimal CPU and memory overhead, which has made it the default log and metrics agent in many Kubernetes distributions and managed cloud platforms.

The project was created in 2014 by Eduardo Silva while working on the Fluentd team at Treasure Data, as a lighter-weight companion to Fluentd for embedded Linux devices and network gateways. It is now a CNCF graduated project under the Fluent project umbrella alongside Fluentd, reflecting its status as production-proven infrastructure software used across thousands of organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Fluent Bit's pipeline is built from inputs, parsers, filters (processors), and outputs, with more than 100 built-in plugins covering sources like container runtimes, systemd, syslog, and HTTP, and destinations like Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Splunk, Amazon S3, CloudWatch, Kafka, Loki, and Fluentd itself.

Recent major versions added native OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP) ingestion and egress, SQL-like stream processing over telemetry records, and support for WebAssembly filter and input plugins, extending Fluent Bit beyond classic log shipping into a general-purpose observability edge agent.

long_form_blocks.heading: Architecture and Deployment
paragraphs: Fluent Bit is event-driven and asynchronous, using chunk-based buffering that can operate in memory or spill to disk, and a tag-based routing engine that lets a single process ingest from many sources and dispatch to many destinations concurrently without duplicating pipelines.

It is most commonly deployed as a per-node DaemonSet in Kubernetes clusters, and is embedded as default or supported tooling in Amazon ECS/Fargate (via FireLens), Amazon EKS, Google Kubernetes Engine, and other managed platforms. Its small binary size and lack of external dependencies also make it well suited to edge computing, IoT gateways, and embedded Linux devices, close to its original 2014 design goal.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Fluent Bit software itself is entirely free and open source under the Apache License 2.0, with no paid tier, license fee, or usage limits for self-hosting and operating it.

Commercial support and managed fleet-management tooling built on top of Fluent Bit is offered by Chronosphere, which acquired Calyptia (the company founded in 2020 by Fluent Bit's creators) in January 2024 and became the project's primary corporate sponsor. Chronosphere's commercial 'Telemetry Pipeline' product and enterprise support pricing are not publicly listed and require contacting its sales team.

faqs.answer: Yes. Fluent Bit is fully open source under the Apache License 2.0, and there is no cost to download, self-host, or modify it for commercial or personal use.

faqs.question: Who created Fluent Bit?
faqs.answer: Fluent Bit was created in 2014 by Eduardo Silva while he was working on the Fluentd team at Treasure Data, as a lighter-weight companion project for embedded and constrained environments.

faqs.question: Is Fluent Bit a CNCF project?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fluent Bit is a CNCF graduated project within the Fluent project umbrella alongside Fluentd, the Cloud Native Computing Foundation's highest maturity classification.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Fluent Bit and Fluentd?
faqs.answer: Fluent Bit is written in C and designed for minimal footprint and high performance in constrained or edge environments, while Fluentd is written in Ruby with C extensions and offers a larger plugin ecosystem better suited to heavier central aggregation. The two are commonly used together.

faqs.question: Who provides commercial support for Fluent Bit?
faqs.answer: Chronosphere, which acquired Calyptia (founded in 2020 by Fluent Bit's original creators) in January 2024, is the primary corporate sponsor of Fluent Bit and offers a commercial Telemetry Pipeline product and enterprise support built on the open-source project.

faqs.question: How does Fluent Bit compare to the OpenTelemetry Collector?
faqs.answer: Fluent Bit now supports native OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP) ingestion and export, allowing it to interoperate with or serve as a lightweight alternative to the OpenTelemetry Collector, particularly in resource-constrained or edge deployments.

enrichment.short_description: Fluentd is a CNCF-graduated, open-source unified logging layer that collects, filters, buffers, and routes JSON-formatted log data between hundreds of heterogeneous sources and destinations using a pluggable architecture.
enrichment.seo_title: Fluentd Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fluentd review 2026: a free, CNCF-graduated unified logging layer with 500+ plugins. See architecture, pricing, pros, cons, and Fluent Bit alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Fluentd is an open-source data collector that functions as a unified logging layer, sitting as middleware between disparate data sources and the backend systems that store, analyze, or alert on that data. Its core design goal is to decouple log producers from log consumers by standardizing on JSON as the common data format, so that any of its input plugins can feed any of its output plugins without custom glue code. Fluentd ships with a small core and a flexible plugin system; the ecosystem today includes more than 500 community- and vendor-maintained plugins covering inputs, filters, buffers, formatters, parsers, and outputs, letting it pull from and push to systems ranging from application logs and syslog to Kafka, Elasticsearch, S3, Kubernetes, and dozens of SaaS analytics and SIEM platforms. Because it treats collecting, filtering, buffering, and routing log data as one coherent pipeline rather than a set of disconnected scripts, Fluentd is commonly used as the backbone for centralized logging in distributed systems, including Kubernetes clusters, microservice fleets, and hybrid on-premises and cloud environments.

The project was created by Sadayuki 'Sada' Furuhashi, a co-founder of Treasure Data, Inc. Fluentd's first commit was made on June 18, 2011, and the project was open-sourced in October 2011 under the Apache License 2.0. It grew out of Treasure Data's own need for a reliable way to collect and forward event data into its cloud data platform, and Treasure Data served as Fluentd's primary corporate sponsor and enterprise support provider in the project's early years. Over the following decade the project expanded well beyond its original sponsor, accumulating meaningful code contributions from more than 1,000 organizations and building a plugin catalog that today spans nearly every common logging, metrics, and event-streaming system in use.

Fluentd's status within the cloud native ecosystem was formalized when the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) accepted it as its sixth hosted project in November 2016, following Kubernetes, Prometheus, and OpenTracing. After roughly two and a half years in the CNCF incubation stage, the foundation announced on April 11, 2019 that Fluentd had graduated, making it the sixth project overall to reach CNCF's graduated maturity level, after Kubernetes, Prometheus, Envoy, CoreDNS, and containerd. Graduation status is CNCF's highest maturity tier and signals that a project has demonstrated broad adoption, a healthy and diverse contributor base, and formal governance, security, and release processes. Fluentd remains part of the broader 'Fluent' project umbrella within the CNCF, which also includes its sibling project Fluent Bit.

Technically, Fluentd's pipeline is built from four plugin categories that are wired together through a configuration file: input plugins that ingest data (tailing files, listening on syslog or HTTP, reading from message queues, and more), filter plugins that transform, enrich, or drop individual events, buffer plugins that queue events for reliable delivery, and output plugins that write the final events to a destination. Every event carries a tag, and Fluentd's match directives use tag patterns, including wildcards, to route events to the correct output configuration, similar in spirit to a rules-based switchboard. Buffering is central to Fluentd's reliability model: buffer plugins group events into chunks, either as in-memory blocks (buf_memory) or on-disk files (buf_file), keyed by any combination of time, tag, or record fields, and each chunk is flushed to its output with configurable retry and backoff behavior. If an output plugin repeatedly fails to write a chunk and exceeds its retry timeout, Fluentd can hand that chunk off to a secondary output as a fallback, which supports at-least-once delivery semantics and helps prevent data loss during downstream outages. This design lets Fluentd survive transient network partitions or backend unavailability without dropping logs, at the cost of additional configuration complexity compared to simpler forwarders.

Fluentd's core is written primarily in Ruby, with performance-critical portions implemented in C, which the project positions as a deliberate trade-off: Ruby makes writing and maintaining the large plugin ecosystem approachable for a wide community of contributors, while the C extensions keep the runtime lightweight. According to the project's own figures, a typical Fluentd instance runs on roughly 30 to 40 megabytes of memory and can process on the order of 13,000 events per second per core, which is efficient for a scripting-language-based agent but measurably heavier than purpose-built, compiled alternatives. For packaging and distribution, the project historically shipped a bundled, production-tuned distribution called td-agent; that distribution has reached end of life and has been succeeded by fluent-package, which the project now recommends for stable, production deployments in place of building from the bare Ruby gem.

Fluentd's pricing model is straightforward because there is no pricing: the software, all of its official plugins, and its documentation are released under the Apache License 2.0 and are free to download, modify, and redistribute, with no paid tiers, seat licenses, or feature gates controlled by the Fluentd project itself. There is no official managed SaaS or hosted version of Fluentd sold directly by the project. Commercial and enterprise support is instead available through the surrounding ecosystem rather than a single vendor: Treasure Data provided early enterprise sponsorship, the Linux Foundation offers a paid online training course covering Fluentd fundamentals, and various observability and logging vendors and consultancies offer paid support, managed deployments, or migration services built around Fluentd. Community support channels, including GitHub discussions, Stack Overflow, Google Groups, and community chat, remain free.

Treasure Data's own corporate history is relevant context for understanding Fluentd's governance, even though Fluentd is not owned by Treasure Data today. Arm Ltd., the UK-based chip design company owned by Japan's SoftBank Group, acquired Treasure Data for approximately 600 million dollars in 2018 to bolster its IoT services portfolio. When Arm's planned sale to Nvidia was abandoned and excluded Arm's IoT Services Group, Treasure Data was spun out in 2021 as an independent, SoftBank-owned company with a fresh 234 million dollar funding round; in 2026 that company rebranded to Treasure AI and repositioned itself from a customer data platform toward an agentic experience platform. Throughout these corporate changes, Fluentd itself has continued to operate as an independently governed, community-driven CNCF project with its own maintainers and release cadence, rather than as an asset that changed hands in these transactions.

Fluentd's target users are primarily DevOps engineers, site reliability engineers, and platform teams who need to unify log collection across heterogeneous, often legacy-plus-cloud-native infrastructure without hand-rolling custom shippers for every data source. The project states that more than 5,000 data-driven companies rely on it in production, with its largest known deployment collecting logs from over 50,000 servers, and it lists adopters including LINE, Amazon Web Services, Microsoft, Atlassian, Nintendo, and Twilio. This makes it a fit both for smaller teams that want a free, flexible log router and for large enterprises that need to normalize log formats across thousands of nodes before feeding them into analytics, search, or SIEM systems.

Fluentd's most direct competitor is its own CNCF sibling, Fluent Bit, which was created by Eduardo Silva and is written in C rather than Ruby, with plugins compiled directly into a single lightweight binary instead of installed separately. Fluent Bit targets resource-constrained and high-throughput environments such as containers and embedded Linux, and can process roughly 10 to 40 times more log volume than Fluentd on comparable hardware depending on the plugin used; it has since become the more widely deployed of the two in new cloud-native installations, with claimed deployments in the billions. Fluent Bit's primary corporate steward changed in 2024 when observability vendor Chronosphere acquired Calyptia, the company founded by Fluent Bit's original creators, making Chronosphere the project's main commercial sponsor. Beyond the Fluent family, Fluentd also competes with Logstash, the JVM-based ingestion component of the Elastic Stack, which offers deep native integration with Elasticsearch and Kibana but carries a heavier memory footprint; Vector, a newer Rust-based observability data pipeline backed by Datadog that emphasizes very high throughput and low resource usage; and Splunk, a proprietary, enterprise-grade log management and SIEM platform that competes more on analytics and compliance features than on raw collection flexibility. Fluentd differentiates itself from all of these primarily through the sheer breadth and maturity of its plugin catalog and its long production track record, rather than through raw performance.

In terms of market position and evolution, Fluentd was for years the default logging agent bundled into many Kubernetes logging stacks, typically deployed as a DaemonSet to tail container logs from every node. As Kubernetes environments have scaled and become more cost- and resource-sensitive, many new deployments have shifted toward Fluent Bit for its smaller footprint, while Fluentd continues to be favored in environments that depend on its larger Ruby plugin ecosystem, complex multi-stage filtering and transformation logic, or existing operational familiarity built up over a decade of production use. Because both projects live under the same CNCF Fluent umbrella and share compatible protocols, many organizations run them together in a hybrid pattern, using Fluent Bit as a lightweight forwarder at the edge and Fluentd as a heavier aggregation and routing layer further downstream, which reflects how the project has evolved from a standalone tool into one half of a complementary logging toolkit rather than a single one-size-fits-all agent.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Fluentd is a free, open-source data collector created to build a unified logging layer for distributed systems. It sits between data sources and backend storage or analytics systems, converting logs into a common JSON format so they can be filtered, buffered, and routed consistently regardless of where they originated.

Originally created in 2011 by Sadayuki Furuhashi at Treasure Data, Fluentd is now a Cloud Native Computing Foundation graduated project, sitting alongside sibling project Fluent Bit under the CNCF Fluent umbrella. It is released under the Apache License 2.0 and has no cost to download, run, or modify.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Fluentd's core strength is its plugin architecture: over 500 input, filter, buffer, and output plugins let it connect to nearly any log source and nearly any destination, from local files and syslog to Kafka, Elasticsearch, S3, and dozens of SaaS platforms.

Its buffering system groups events into memory- or file-based chunks keyed by tag, time, or record fields, with configurable retries and a secondary-output fallback, giving it at-least-once delivery semantics that protect against data loss during backend outages.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Fluentd Works
paragraphs: Every event that flows through Fluentd carries a tag, and match directives with wildcard support route each tagged event to the correct output configuration, similar to a rules-based switchboard for log data.

The core is written primarily in Ruby with performance-sensitive parts in C, giving a typical instance a footprint of roughly 30 to 40 megabytes of memory while processing around 13,000 events per second per core. Production deployments are packaged as fluent-package, the successor to the now end-of-life td-agent distribution.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Fluentd itself has no pricing tiers, license fees, or paid feature gates; the software and its official plugins are entirely free and open source under Apache 2.0.

There is no official managed SaaS from the Fluentd project. Optional paid support comes from the surrounding ecosystem, such as Linux Foundation training courses and enterprise support offered by vendors and consultancies, while community help remains free via GitHub, Stack Overflow, and community chat channels.

long_form_blocks.heading: Fluentd vs. Fluent Bit and Other Alternatives
paragraphs: Fluent Bit, Fluentd's CNCF sibling project, is written in C, embeds its plugins directly into a single binary, and can process roughly 10 to 40 times more log volume on the same hardware, making it the more common choice for new, resource-constrained, cloud-native deployments. Chronosphere became Fluent Bit's primary corporate sponsor after acquiring Calyptia in 2024.

Other alternatives include Logstash, the JVM-based ingestion layer of the Elastic Stack; Vector, a Rust-based pipeline backed by Datadog; and Splunk, a proprietary enterprise log management and SIEM platform. Fluentd differentiates itself mainly through the breadth and maturity of its plugin ecosystem and long production history rather than raw throughput.
