faqs.question: Is Fluentd free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fluentd and its official plugins are fully open source under the Apache License 2.0, with no license fees or paid tiers controlled by the project itself.

faqs.question: Who created Fluentd?
faqs.answer: Fluentd was created by Sadayuki Furuhashi, a co-founder of Treasure Data, with its first commit made on June 18, 2011, and the project open-sourced in October 2011.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Fluentd and Fluent Bit?
faqs.answer: Fluentd is written in Ruby and C with a large, community-driven plugin ecosystem best suited to complex routing and transformation, while Fluent Bit is written in C, embeds plugins into a single binary, and is optimized for high throughput in resource-constrained environments like containers, processing roughly 10 to 40 times more log volume on the same hardware.

faqs.question: Is Fluentd still actively maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fluentd is a CNCF graduated project, a status it reached in April 2019, and continues to receive updates, with production deployments now recommended to use fluent-package, the successor to the retired td-agent distribution.

faqs.question: Does Fluentd offer official enterprise support?
faqs.answer: Fluentd itself does not sell a managed service or support contract. Paid support and training are available through the ecosystem, including a Linux Foundation training course and support from various logging and observability vendors.

faqs.question: Who owns Fluentd today?
faqs.answer: Fluentd is governed as an independent, community-run CNCF project rather than owned by a single company. It originated at Treasure Data, which was later acquired by Arm in 2018 and spun out as an independent SoftBank-owned company in 2021, but that corporate history does not give any single company ownership of the Fluentd project.

enrichment.seo_title: Fluidd Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fluidd is a free, open-source Klipper web UI for 3D printers. See how it works with Moonraker, its features, install options, and how it compares to Mainsail.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Fluidd is a free and open-source web interface built specifically for Klipper, the high-performance 3D printer firmware that offloads motion planning to a general-purpose computer such as a Raspberry Pi. Rather than functioning as a standalone application, Fluidd is a browser-based control panel: it renders in any modern web browser on a desktop, tablet, or phone and talks to a printer over the local network, giving the user live temperature graphs, G-code file management, macro execution, webcam feeds, and full manual control of a Klipper-based machine without needing to install any native software on the client device. The project is maintained by the fluidd-core organization on GitHub and is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPL-3.0), which means the full source code is publicly available, modifiable, and redistributable at no cost.

The fluidd-core/fluidd repository was created on September 15, 2020, placing the project's origin firmly in the early growth phase of the Klipper firmware ecosystem, a period when the DIY CoreXY printer community centered around the Voron Design project was rapidly expanding and looking for a faster, more modern alternative to the then-dominant OctoPrint interface. Community accounts credit developer Craig Bassett, known in the community by the handle 'cadriel,' as the project's originator, with Fluidd emerging specifically to serve builders in the Voron community who wanted a snappier, more visually polished dashboard than what existed at the time. Since its creation the project has grown to roughly 1,800 GitHub stars and around 590 forks, with a small, rotating group of volunteer maintainers and community contributors pushing regular releases; the changelog shows a near-monthly release cadence in recent years after a quieter stretch around 2021 into early 2022. As of mid-2026 the project's latest tagged release is v1.37.2, reflecting continued active maintenance rather than a one-time community snapshot.

Technically, Fluidd sits at the top of a three-layer stack that is standard across the modern Klipper ecosystem. At the bottom is Klipper itself, the firmware that runs partly on the printer's microcontroller and partly as a Python process on a host computer, translating G-code into precisely timed stepper motor pulses. In the middle sits Moonraker, a companion API server written in Python by developer Eric Callahan (known as 'Arksine') that exposes Klipper's internal state and functionality over HTTP and WebSocket connections, handles file uploads, manages update checking, and provides hooks for third-party integrations. Fluidd itself is the top layer: a single-page application written in Vue.js and TypeScript (the GitHub repository is roughly 65 percent Vue and 34 percent TypeScript by code volume) that consumes Moonraker's API to render the printer's live state, accept user commands, and stream G-code visualizations, temperature charts, and webcam feeds back to the browser. Because Fluidd is purely a front end that speaks to Moonraker over the network, a single Fluidd installation can be pointed at, and can switch between, multiple independent Klipper printers, and the interface itself can be hosted in several different ways depending on user preference.

Installation is flexible by design. The most common path for new users is KIAUH, the community-maintained 'Klipper Installation and Update Helper' script, which installs and updates Klipper, Moonraker, and Fluidd together from a single menu-driven tool. Users who prefer containerized deployments can pull official Docker images, published as both a standard 'fluidd' image running on port 80 and an unprivileged 'fluidd-unprivileged' variant on port 8080, which is useful when one Fluidd instance needs to serve several printers. For manual setups, the project publishes a pre-built fluidd.zip archive with every GitHub release that can be extracted and served by any static HTTP server such as NGINX, and developers who want to build from source need Node.js v24.x, Git, and the pnpm package manager. There is also a hosted browser version at app.fluidd.xyz that runs entirely client-side and connects directly to a printer's local Moonraker instance without routing data through an external server. A dedicated Raspberry Pi OS Lite image called FluiddPi, which bundled Klipper, Moonraker, Fluidd, and webcam support pre-configured, was offered as a turnkey option but is now flagged by the project as unmaintained, with KIAUH positioned as the recommended path instead.

On pricing, Fluidd has no commercial tier at all: it is entirely free to download, install, and use, with no feature gating, license keys, or subscription requirements of any kind. The project sustains itself through voluntary donations via GitHub Sponsors and support from hardware sponsors in the 3D printing space, most notably LDO Motors, which the project lists as an official sponsor. There is no venture funding, no parent company, and no headquarters, consistent with Fluidd's identity as a community-driven open-source project rather than a commercial software vendor; the maintainers explicitly describe the project as 'built by volunteers.'

Fluidd's target audience is squarely the DIY and enthusiast 3D printing community: hobbyists running self-built or heavily modified printers, members of the Voron Design and broader CoreXY builder community, and small-scale makerspaces or print farms that need to monitor and control several Klipper-based machines from one browser tab. Because it requires a working Klipper and Moonraker setup underneath it, Fluidd is not aimed at casual users of stock, closed-firmware consumer printers; it assumes a degree of technical comfort with flashing firmware, editing configuration files, and troubleshooting a Linux-based host such as a Raspberry Pi.

Within the Klipper interface ecosystem, Fluidd's most direct competitor is Mainsail, another free, GPL-licensed, Vue-based web UI that talks to the same Moonraker API and is frequently installed through the same KIAUH tool. Community comparisons generally position Mainsail as the more feature-rich and customizable of the two, offering elements like a shared macro store and more extensive dashboard configuration, while Fluidd is more often described as the lighter and more responsive option, with a cleaner interface that some users find better suited to lower-powered single-board computers. The older and more established alternative in this space is OctoPrint, a Python-based print server with its own plugin ecosystem that predates Klipper and can also control non-Klipper, Marlin-based printers, but which many Klipper users find heavier and less responsive than either Fluidd or Mainsail for pure Klipper workflows. Because all of these tools are free and can be installed side by side or swapped via KIAUH, the practical choice between them tends to come down to personal preference for interface density and customization rather than cost or lock-in.

Beyond its core interface, Fluidd has built integrations with several tools that have become fixtures of the advanced Klipper modding scene, including Kalico (a Klipper fork with additional experimental features), Happy Hare (an automated multi-material filament-changing system), Beacon (an eddy-current bed-probing sensor), and Obico (an AI-based print failure detection service), reflecting Fluidd's positioning as infrastructure for the more technically adventurous end of the 3D printing hobbyist market rather than a beginner-oriented, closed appliance experience. The project also ships multi-language localization and supports simultaneous MJPEG, HLS, and WebRTC webcam feeds, letting users monitor multiple camera angles of a single print or printer farm at once. Taken together, Fluidd represents a mature example of a narrowly scoped, community-funded open-source project that has, since its 2020 origin in the Voron builder community, become one of the two or three de facto standard control interfaces for the Klipper firmware ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Fluidd is a free, open-source web interface purpose-built for Klipper, the 3D printer firmware that moves motion planning off the printer's microcontroller and onto a companion computer such as a Raspberry Pi. Rather than being installed on the printer itself, Fluidd runs as a Vue.js and TypeScript single-page application that loads in any modern browser and communicates with Moonraker, the API server that bridges Klipper and web-based front ends, over the local network.

The project is maintained by the fluidd-core organization on GitHub, with the underlying repository created in September 2020 and now carrying a GPL-3.0 license, around 1,800 stars, and roughly 590 forks. It is entirely volunteer-driven, with no parent company, headquarters, or venture funding behind it, and it is sustained through community donations via GitHub Sponsors and support from hardware sponsors such as LDO Motors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Fluidd's dashboard is responsive across desktop, tablet, and mobile screens and includes an interactive G-code viewer for previewing toolpaths, live thermal charts for extruders and the heated bed, and support for multiple simultaneous webcam feeds over MJPEG, HLS, or WebRTC. File management lets users upload, organize, and preview G-code, while a macro panel exposes custom Klipper commands for quick access during printing.

Because Fluidd is a pure front end for the Moonraker API, a single installation can manage and switch between several independent Klipper printers, which makes it a practical choice for small print farms as well as single-printer hobbyists. The project also integrates with popular Klipper-ecosystem add-ons including Kalico, Happy Hare, Beacon, and Obico, and ships built-in multi-language localization and print-history tracking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Fluidd has no paid tier, license key, or subscription of any kind: the full application is free to download, self-host, and use under the GPL-3.0 open-source license. There is no feature gating between a 'free' and 'pro' version because there is only one version.

Ongoing development is funded through voluntary community support rather than product revenue, primarily via GitHub Sponsors, along with backing from hardware sponsors in the 3D printing space such as LDO Motors. Users who want to install Fluidd can choose among several free paths, including the KIAUH installer, official Docker images, a manually deployed release archive, a source build with Node.js and pnpm, or the hosted app.fluidd.xyz browser client.

features.title: Responsive multi-device dashboard
features.description: A single interface that adapts cleanly to desktop, tablet, and mobile browsers, so a printer can be monitored and controlled from any device on the network.

features.title: G-code visualization

features.title: Real-time thermal monitoring
features.description: Live, historical charts of extruder, bed, and chamber temperatures for tracking heating behavior and diagnosing thermal issues.

features.title: Multi-webcam support
features.description: Simultaneous camera feeds over MJPEG, HLS, and WebRTC protocols for visual print monitoring from one or more angles.

features.title: File and macro management
features.description: Upload, organize, and preview G-code files, and trigger custom Klipper macros directly from the dashboard.

features.title: Print history tracking
features.description: A log of completed, canceled, and failed prints with duration and filament usage details for reviewing past jobs.

features.title: Multi-printer management
features.description: One Fluidd instance can connect to and switch between several independent Klipper-based printers over Moonraker.

features.title: Ecosystem integrations
features.description: Built-in support for community Klipper add-ons such as Kalico, Happy Hare automated filament changing, Beacon eddy-current probing, and Obico AI failure detection.

pros: Completely free and open source under GPL-3.0, with no paid tiers, license keys, or feature gating.
Lightweight and responsive, frequently cited by users as faster than Mainsail or OctoPrint on low-power boards such as the Raspberry Pi.
Deep roots in the Voron and Klipper modding community, with active integrations for popular mods like Happy Hare and Beacon.
Multiple install paths, including KIAUH, Docker, manual deployment, source build, and a hosted browser app, suit both beginners and advanced tinkerers.
cons: Offers fewer built-in customization options than Mainsail, such as no shared macro store.
Requires an already-working Klipper and Moonraker setup; Fluidd alone cannot control a printer.
No commercial support or service-level agreement; help comes from volunteer maintainers via Discord and GitHub.
The FluiddPi Raspberry Pi image is no longer actively maintained, so new users are steered toward KIAUH instead.

faqs.question: Is Fluidd free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fluidd is entirely free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license, with no paid plans. Development is funded through voluntary donations via GitHub Sponsors and hardware sponsors such as LDO Motors.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Fluidd, Klipper, and Moonraker?
faqs.answer: Klipper is the firmware that controls the printer's motion; Moonraker is the API server that exposes Klipper's state and functions over the network; Fluidd is the web-based front end that connects to Moonraker to give users a visual dashboard and controls.

faqs.question: Does Fluidd work with OctoPrint?
faqs.answer: Fluidd is an alternative to OctoPrint rather than an add-on for it. Most users choose one interface for day-to-day printer control, though Moonraker can be configured to coexist with an OctoPrint installation on the same host in some setups.

faqs.question: How do I install Fluidd?
faqs.answer: The most common method is the community KIAUH installer, which sets up Klipper, Moonraker, and Fluidd together. Alternatives include official Docker images, a manually deployed release archive served over NGINX, building from source with Node.js and pnpm, or using the hosted browser client at app.fluidd.xyz.

faqs.question: Is Fluidd or Mainsail better?
faqs.answer: Both are free, open-source, GPL-licensed web interfaces that use the same Moonraker API, so neither is objectively better. Mainsail generally offers more built-in customization and a community macro store, while Fluidd is often described as lighter and more responsive, particularly on lower-powered hardware.

faqs.question: Who maintains Fluidd?
faqs.answer: Fluidd is maintained by the fluidd-core organization, a group of volunteer developers and community contributors. The project originated in the Voron 3D printer builder community, with the GitHub repository created in September 2020, and continues to receive regular updates from its maintainers and open-source contributors.

use_cases.title: Home 3D printing monitoring and control
use_cases.description: Hobbyists running self-built or upgraded Klipper printers use Fluidd to watch print progress, adjust settings on the fly, and review temperature history from a browser on any device.

use_cases.title: Voron and custom CoreXY printer builds
use_cases.description: Builders in the Voron Design community and other DIY CoreXY projects use Fluidd for multi-tool-head visualization and deep integration with mods like Happy Hare and Beacon.
use_cases.audience: DIY printer builders and the Voron community

use_cases.title: Small print farm management
use_cases.description: Makerspaces and small print farm operators run a single Fluidd instance to monitor and switch between several Klipper-based printers from one dashboard.

Self-hosted on your own hardware with no usage limits
Multi-printer management from a single installation
Community support via GitHub and Discord

enrichment.short_description: Flux, also known as FluxCD, is a CNCF Graduated, open source GitOps continuous delivery tool that continuously reconciles Kubernetes clusters with the desired state declared in Git, Helm, and OCI repositories.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source (optional paid Enterprise support add-on)
enrichment.seo_title: Flux (FluxCD) Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Flux (FluxCD) is a CNCF Graduated, open source GitOps tool for Kubernetes. See its GitOps Toolkit architecture, pricing, pros, cons, and Argo CD alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Flux, commonly referred to as FluxCD, is an open source GitOps continuous delivery and progressive delivery toolkit purpose-built for Kubernetes. At its core, Flux implements the GitOps operating model: the desired state of applications, cluster configuration, infrastructure manifests, and even monitoring dashboards is declared as code in a Git repository, an OCI artifact registry, or an object storage bucket, and a set of in-cluster controllers continuously and automatically reconciles the live state of the cluster to match that declared source of truth. Rather than pushing changes to clusters via kubectl or CI pipelines with direct cluster credentials, Flux pulls changes from the source, compares them against what is actually running, and applies the difference, which removes the need to expose cluster credentials to external systems and gives teams a full, auditable Git history of every change ever made to their infrastructure.

Flux's origins trace back to Weaveworks, a London-based cloud native infrastructure company founded in 2014 by Alexis Richardson and Matthias Radestock. It was Weaveworks, and specifically Richardson, who coined the term 'GitOps' in an August 2017 blog post to describe operational patterns the company's engineers had already been practicing internally, patterns influenced by site reliability engineering disciplines popularized at Google. Flux itself was released as an open source project by Weaveworks not long after, initially as a single monolithic operator, now referred to retrospectively as Flux v1, that watched a Git repository and applied manifests to a cluster. As Kubernetes GitOps use cases matured, the Flux maintainers undertook a full architectural rewrite, splitting the single operator into a composable set of specialized Kubernetes controllers, an initiative known as the GitOps Toolkit. This rewrite became Flux v2, and it reached general availability in mid-2023, with the CNCF publishing the GA announcement in July 2023 as a major project milestone.

Flux's standing in the cloud native ecosystem was formally recognized well before that GA milestone. The project entered the CNCF Sandbox, progressed to Incubation, and on November 30, 2022, the Cloud Native Computing Foundation announced that Flux had graduated, becoming the 18th CNCF project to reach Graduated maturity, the foundation's highest tier reserved for projects that have demonstrated thriving adoption, a documented, structured governance process, and successful completion of a third-party security audit. Graduation placed Flux alongside Kubernetes itself, Prometheus, and its principal rival in the GitOps space, Argo CD, which graduated around the same period, cementing GitOps as a mainstream, foundation-endorsed pattern for Kubernetes operations rather than a single-vendor concept.

A pivotal moment in Flux's history came in February 2024, when Weaveworks, the company that had originated the project and continued to employ many of its core maintainers, announced it was shutting down. Founder and CEO Alexis Richardson disclosed the closure in a public post, citing a promising acquisition or investment process that collapsed late in negotiations, following a period of lumpy revenue growth and a volatile cash position typical of open-core infrastructure startups. Because Flux had already been donated to and governed by the CNCF as an independent, vendor-neutral project with its own Technical Steering Committee, Weaveworks' closure did not threaten the code, the trademark, or the roadmap, though it did remove the project's largest single commercial backer and raised real questions in the community about long-term maintainer funding. Those questions were answered within weeks: ControlPlane, a Kubernetes and cloud native security consultancy, announced in March 2024 that it was hiring several of the former Weaveworks core maintainers, including Stefan Prodan, who had been a Principal Engineer at Weaveworks and one of Flux's most prolific contributors, to continue leading Flux development. ControlPlane has since publicly reaffirmed its multi-year commitment to funding Flux maintainers and now sells an annual 'ControlPlane Enterprise for Flux CD' subscription that bundles a commercially licensed version of the Flux Operator with support and professional services, using that revenue to directly sustain the open source project.

Technically, Flux v2 is delivered as a set of independent, purpose-built controllers collectively branded the GitOps Toolkit, each exposing its own Kubernetes Custom Resource Definitions. The source-controller is responsible for acquiring and verifying content from Git repositories, OCI repositories, Helm repositories and charts, and plain storage buckets, producing versioned artifacts that other controllers consume. The kustomize-controller reconciles the Kustomization custom resource, applying Kustomize-built manifests from a source to the cluster and continuously drift-detecting and correcting any manual changes. The helm-controller manages the HelmRelease custom resource, treating an entire Helm chart installation, including values overrides and upgrade strategy, as a declarative, Git-tracked object. The notification-controller centralizes event forwarding and alerting, dispatching reconciliation events to Slack, Microsoft Teams, Discord, and other providers, and exposing webhook receivers so external CI systems can trigger reconciliation. Finally, the image-reflector-controller scans configured container registries for new tags while the image-automation-controller commits updated image references directly back to the Git repository, enabling fully automated image update workflows without a human editing YAML by hand. Because each controller is an independent Kubernetes operator, teams can install only the pieces they need, which keeps Flux's resource footprint small compared to more monolithic platforms.

Flux's primary audience is platform engineering, DevOps, and site reliability teams operating Kubernetes at any scale, from single clusters to large multi-tenant, multi-cluster fleets spanning multiple clouds and regions. It integrates natively with Kubernetes RBAC through service account impersonation, giving each tenant or team least-privilege access scoped to its own namespace or Git source, and it works out of the box with Cluster API for fleet-wide cluster lifecycle management. Organizations that have publicly discussed running Flux in production include Deutsche Telekom, Microsoft Azure teams, and a range of financial services, healthcare, and government deployments, including reported usage within United States Department of Defense environments, reflecting Flux's reputation for security-conscious, pull-based, low-privilege operation suited to regulated and air-gapped environments.

Flux's most direct and frequently compared competitor is Argo CD, another CNCF Graduated GitOps project originally built at Intuit. The two tools solve overlapping problems but take different architectural approaches: Argo CD is typically deployed as a centralized, hub-and-spoke control plane with a full-featured web UI, native single sign-on, and an application-centric mental model well suited to developer self-service, while Flux ships as a decentralized set of lightweight controllers running independently inside each target cluster, favoring CLI and API-driven workflows, a smaller resource footprint, and no mandatory central dashboard or database. Flux is also frequently discussed alongside older or broader continuous delivery tools such as Jenkins X, which layers GitOps concepts on top of Jenkins pipelines, and Spinnaker, a multi-cloud continuous delivery platform originally from Netflix that predates the GitOps term and takes a push-based, pipeline-centric approach rather than Flux's pull-based reconciliation model. Flux differentiates itself through its toolkit philosophy, letting teams compose exactly the controllers they need, its close technical integration with Flagger for progressive delivery patterns like canary releases and automated rollbacks, and its emphasis on being an embeddable engine for platform teams building their own internal developer platforms rather than an end-user-facing product.

Commercially, Flux itself carries no license fee. The core project, including all GitOps Toolkit controllers, is Apache 2.0 licensed and free to self-host on any Kubernetes cluster, with community support available through GitHub Discussions, a CNCF-hosted Slack workspace, and public working group meetings. The only paid layer sits on top of the open source project: ControlPlane's Enterprise for Flux CD subscription, which wraps a commercially licensed, AGPL-based Flux Operator with hardened lifecycle automation, security assurance, and direct professional services from the maintainer team, priced on a custom annual subscription basis rather than a published price list. This structure, free core plus an optional vendor-backed enterprise layer, has become the primary sustainability model for the project following Weaveworks' exit, and it mirrors the funding pattern used by several other CNCF Graduated projects that rely on a small number of committed corporate sponsors rather than a single founding company.

Looking at its broader trajectory, Flux has moved from being the flagship product of a single venture-backed startup to being a foundation-governed, multi-vendor-supported piece of core cloud native infrastructure, a transition that is itself a notable case study in open source project sustainability. Its continued CNCF Graduated status, active release cadence, and renewed corporate backing through ControlPlane suggest the project's long-term future is stable independent of any single company's fate, and its architectural emphasis on small, composable, pull-based controllers keeps it particularly well positioned for edge computing, air-gapped, and highly regulated Kubernetes deployments where centralized control planes and inbound network requirements are undesirable.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Flux, also known as FluxCD, is a CNCF Graduated, open source GitOps continuous delivery tool for Kubernetes. It continuously compares the live state of a cluster against a desired state declared in Git, Helm repositories, or OCI artifacts, and automatically reconciles any drift, so that Git effectively becomes the single, auditable source of truth for everything running on the cluster.

The project was originally created by Weaveworks, the London-based company that coined the term 'GitOps' in 2017, and was later fully rewritten as a modular set of Kubernetes controllers called the GitOps Toolkit, released as Flux v2. Flux graduated within the CNCF on November 30, 2022, becoming the 18th project to reach that highest maturity tier. After Weaveworks shut down in February 2024, the Kubernetes security consultancy ControlPlane hired several of the project's core maintainers to keep development and commercial support alive.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Flux's functionality is delivered through independent GitOps Toolkit controllers: source-controller acquires content from Git, OCI, Helm, and bucket sources; kustomize-controller and helm-controller apply Kustomize manifests and Helm charts respectively; notification-controller handles alerting and webhook receivers; and the image-reflector-controller and image-automation-controller together detect new container image tags and commit updated references back to Git automatically.

Because each controller is a discrete Kubernetes operator with its own Custom Resource Definitions, teams can install only the components they need, keeping the resource footprint small. Flux supports multi-tenancy through Kubernetes RBAC impersonation, works across multi-cluster and multi-cloud fleets alongside Cluster API, and pairs with the companion Flagger project to enable progressive delivery patterns such as canary releases, blue-green deployments, and automated rollback based on metrics.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Flux project is entirely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no feature gating, seat limits, or usage caps for self-hosted deployments. Support for the free tier comes from the community through GitHub Discussions, a CNCF Slack workspace, and public maintainer meetings.

The only paid offering sits on top of the free project: ControlPlane sells an annual 'Enterprise for Flux CD' subscription that bundles a commercially licensed, hardened version of the Flux Operator with vendor SLAs and professional services, priced on a custom basis per organization rather than published tiers. Revenue from that subscription is used to directly fund the maintainers who continue developing the open source project.

features.title: Git as the Single Source of Truth
features.description: The desired state of applications, cluster configuration, and infrastructure is declared in Git, and Flux continuously reconciles the live cluster to match it, giving every change a full, auditable history.

features.title: Modular GitOps Toolkit Controllers
features.description: Source-controller, kustomize-controller, helm-controller, notification-controller, image-reflector-controller, and image-automation-controller each run as an independent Kubernetes operator with its own Custom Resource Definitions.

features.title: Native Kustomize and Helm Support
features.description: Deploy raw manifests through Kustomize overlays via the Kustomization resource or full Helm chart releases via the HelmRelease resource, without requiring extra tooling layered on top.

features.title: Multi-Tenancy and Multi-Cluster Fleets
features.description: RBAC impersonation isolates tenants at the namespace and source level, and integration with Cluster API supports fleet-wide provisioning and management across many clusters and clouds.

features.title: Automated Container Image Updates
features.description: The image-reflector-controller scans configured registries for new tags and the image-automation-controller commits updated image references back to Git automatically, closing the loop without manual YAML edits.

features.title: Progressive Delivery with Flagger
features.description: Pairs with the companion Flagger project to run canary releases, blue-green deployments, and A/B tests, automatically rolling back based on defined health metrics.

features.title: OCI, Bucket, and Air-Gapped Sources
features.description: Beyond Git, Flux can pull artifacts from OCI registries and object storage buckets, making it well suited to air-gapped and network-restricted environments.

features.title: Notifications and Webhook Receivers
features.description: The notification-controller forwards reconciliation events to Slack, Microsoft Teams, Discord, and other providers, and exposes webhook receivers so external CI systems can trigger a sync.

pros: Fully free, Apache 2.0 licensed core with no paywalled features or usage limits for self-hosted use
Lightweight, independently-composable controller architecture that keeps resource usage small, favoring edge and resource-constrained clusters
CNCF Graduated status backed by a broad, vendor-neutral maintainer community rather than a single company
Deep native integration with Kustomize, Helm, and Kubernetes RBAC without requiring a separate application layer or database
cons: No built-in web UI in the core project, so visual, single pane of glass management requires separate third-party tooling
Steeper initial learning curve than dashboard-first tools for teams that expect an out-of-the-box graphical interface
Weaveworks, the project's original commercial backer, shut down in 2024, which briefly created uncertainty about long-term enterprise support until ControlPlane stepped in
Smaller name recognition and marketing presence among application developers compared with Argo CD, which ships a more polished default UI

faqs.question: What is Flux (FluxCD) used for?
faqs.answer: Flux is used to continuously deploy and manage Kubernetes applications and cluster configuration using GitOps: it watches Git, Helm, or OCI sources and automatically reconciles the cluster to match the declared desired state.

faqs.question: Is Flux free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The core Flux project and all GitOps Toolkit controllers are open source under the Apache 2.0 license and free to self-host with no seat or usage limits. An optional paid ControlPlane Enterprise subscription adds vendor support and a hardened Flux Operator.

faqs.question: Did the Weaveworks shutdown affect the Flux project?
faqs.answer: Weaveworks, the company that originally created Flux and coined the term GitOps, shut down in February 2024. Flux itself was unaffected because it is governed independently by the CNCF, and ControlPlane subsequently hired several of the former core maintainers, including Stefan Prodan, to continue the project.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Flux and Argo CD?
faqs.answer: Both are CNCF Graduated GitOps tools, but Argo CD typically runs as a centralized control plane with a full web UI and single sign-on, while Flux runs as a decentralized set of lightweight controllers inside each cluster, favoring a smaller footprint, CLI and API-driven workflows, and no mandatory central dashboard.

faqs.question: What is the GitOps Toolkit?
faqs.answer: The GitOps Toolkit is the set of composable Kubernetes controllers that make up Flux v2, including source-controller, kustomize-controller, helm-controller, notification-controller, image-reflector-controller, and image-automation-controller, each exposing its own Custom Resource Definitions.

faqs.question: Is Flux a CNCF Graduated project?
faqs.answer: Yes. Flux graduated within the Cloud Native Computing Foundation on November 30, 2022, becoming the 18th project to reach Graduated maturity, the foundation's highest level of project trust and stability.

use_cases.title: Kubernetes Continuous Delivery for Platform Teams
use_cases.description: Platform engineers use Flux to automatically deploy applications and configuration to Kubernetes clusters directly from Git, removing manual kubectl operations and giving every change a reviewable pull request history.

use_cases.title: Multi-Cluster and Multi-Tenant Fleet Management
use_cases.description: Enterprises operating many Kubernetes clusters across regions or clouds use Flux's RBAC impersonation and Cluster API integration to manage tenant isolation and fleet-wide configuration from a shared set of Git repositories.

use_cases.description: DevOps and SRE teams combine Flux's image automation controllers with the Flagger project to automatically roll out new container image versions as canary releases and roll back automatically if health metrics degrade.

enrichment.short_description: Fly.io is a developer-focused public cloud that runs applications and databases in Firecracker microVMs across dozens of regions worldwide, letting teams deploy full-stack apps close to their users via a global Anycast network.
enrichment.starting_price: Pay-as-you-go from about $2 per month per shared-cpu-1x machine; new accounts get a limited free trial (2 VM hours or 7 days, whichever comes first) before a credit card is required
enrichment.seo_title: Fly.io Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fly.io review 2026: see how Firecracker microVMs, global Anycast networking, and usage-based pricing compare to Render, Railway, Heroku, and Vercel.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Fly.io is a developer-focused public cloud platform that lets engineering teams deploy full-stack applications, APIs, and databases in regions close to their end users, rather than in a single centralized data center. Instead of the traditional model of picking one cloud region and serving all global traffic from it, Fly.io packages an application into a container image and runs that image as one or more Firecracker microVMs distributed across a worldwide network of physical servers. The company describes itself as building a new kind of public cloud that is easy to deploy on for individual developers while remaining powerful enough to grow into production infrastructure for larger teams, positioning itself as an alternative to both legacy platforms-as-a-service like Heroku and to hand-rolled deployments on hyperscale clouds such as AWS.

The company was founded in 2017 by Kurt Mackey, Jerome Gravel-Niquet, and Michael Dwan. Kurt Mackey serves as chief executive officer and Jerome Gravel-Niquet has held the title of developer and chief technology officer. Fly.io is headquartered in Chicago, Illinois, in the United States, though its engineering and support staff are distributed across many countries, including Canada, the United Kingdom, Australia, Brazil, Germany, and Poland. The company emerged from earlier work the founders had done on WordPress hosting and edge networking, and it went through Y Combinator before pivoting toward the general-purpose application deployment platform it is today. Since its founding, Fly.io has grown into a team of roughly fifty to sixty people supporting a global infrastructure footprint.

Fly.io has raised meaningful venture capital to build out its physical server network. According to Crunchbase and reporting from Intel Capital, the company raised a 12 million dollar Series A round led by Intel Capital, followed by a 25 million dollar Series B round led by Andreessen Horowitz, with participation from Dell Technologies Capital, Initialized Capital, and individual investors including PlanetScale CEO Sam Lambert. In 2023, Fly.io closed a 70 million dollar Series C round led by EQT Ventures, which valued the company at approximately 397 million dollars. Across its funding history the company has raised roughly 110.5 million dollars in total, with Y Combinator among its early backers. This capital has been used substantially to buy and lease physical hardware in data centers around the world, since Fly.io's core value proposition depends on owning bare-metal capacity in many geographic locations rather than reselling a single hyperscaler's regions.

Technically, Fly.io's architecture centers on Firecracker, the open-source virtual machine monitor originally built by AWS for Lambda and Fargate. Firecracker creates lightweight, secure microVMs that boot in a fraction of a second and provide strong hardware-level isolation between tenants, which lets Fly.io pack many customer workloads onto shared physical servers without the security or noisy-neighbor problems associated with plain containers. Each physical server in the Fly.io fleet typically has between 8 and 32 CPU cores and 32 to 256 gigabytes of RAM, and customer applications are scheduled onto these servers as individual Fly Machines, which are the fundamental compute unit on the platform. Developers describe their application and its resource requirements in a fly.toml configuration file and use the flyctl command-line tool to build, deploy, scale, and manage Machines, or they can create and control Machines programmatically through the Fly Machines API for more dynamic, ephemeral workloads such as per-user sandboxes or agent runtimes.

Networking is handled through a combination of BGP Anycast routing and a custom Rust-based proxy called fly-proxy. Fly.io announces its IP address ranges from every data center simultaneously, so a request from a user in Tokyo, Sao Paulo, or Amsterdam is automatically routed by internet infrastructure to the nearest Fly.io data center rather than to one fixed origin. Once a connection arrives, fly-proxy terminates TLS, matches the request to the correct customer application, and proxies the traffic to the nearest available microVM running that app. When the closest physical location does not have a running instance of an application, Fly.io backhauls the connection over an encrypted WireGuard tunnel to a data center that does, which keeps latency low while still allowing applications to run in a smaller number of regions than the full network footprint. Fly.io also provides a private WireGuard-based network by default for every organization, letting Machines communicate with each other and with developers' local machines securely without being exposed to the public internet. On top of compute and networking, Fly.io offers Fly Postgres and newer managed Postgres offerings for relational databases, persistent Volumes for durable block storage attached to Machines, and integrations with third-party data services such as Tigris object storage and Upstash Redis.

Fly.io's pricing model is entirely usage-based rather than built around flat subscription tiers. Compute is billed per second based on the CPU and memory preset selected for each Machine, split broadly into shared-cpu and dedicated performance-cpu categories, with additional memory billed per gigabyte on top of the base preset. Storage is billed through persistent Volumes at a per-gigabyte monthly rate, with volume snapshots billed separately after a small free monthly allowance. Networking costs include a monthly fee for dedicated IPv4 addresses, small per-hostname fees for SSL certificates beyond a free allotment, and outbound data transfer charged per gigabyte at rates that vary by region group. Fly.io also sells a managed Fly Kubernetes offering billed at a flat monthly rate per cluster plus underlying compute and storage costs, and offers optional paid support plans ranging from tens of dollars to several thousand dollars per month for organizations that need guaranteed response times. Notably, Fly.io removed its long-standing free allowances, previously bundled into Hobby, Launch, and Scale plans that included a handful of always-on shared-cpu Machines and a bandwidth allotment, effective October 2024. Customers who purchased those legacy plans before the cutoff can remain on them as long as they do not switch to the new pay-as-you-go billing, but all new signups now receive only a minimal trial of about 2 VM hours or 7 days before a credit card and usage-based billing become mandatory. In practice, the cheapest always-on application on Fly.io tends to cost somewhere in the low single digits of dollars per month.

Fly.io's target audience is professional developers and small-to-medium engineering teams building applications, APIs, and backend services that benefit from being close to users or from fine-grained control over container-based deployment, including real-time applications, multiplayer backends, AI agent sandboxes, and internationally distributed SaaS products. It is frequently discussed alongside a cluster of modern deployment platforms that emerged partly in response to Heroku's stagnation and eventual shutdown of its free tier, including Render, Railway, and Vercel, as well as being compared to doing the equivalent work manually on AWS. Render is generally viewed as the closest like-for-like Heroku replacement, offering fixed monthly pricing per service and a simpler mental model at the cost of Fly.io's global-edge flexibility. Railway emphasizes an especially smooth developer experience with automatic framework detection and one-click GitHub deploys, appealing to indie developers who want Fly.io-style usage-based billing without managing regions and Machines directly. Vercel focuses primarily on frontend frameworks, most notably Next.js, and serverless functions rather than long-running backend services or databases, which puts it in a more adjacent category than a direct substitute. Fly.io differentiates itself from all of these primarily through its emphasis on raw infrastructure control combined with global distribution: because applications run as ordinary Docker-compatible containers inside Firecracker microVMs, developers get low-level control over runtime behavior, custom networking, and multi-region placement that abstracted platforms-as-a-service typically do not expose, while still avoiding the operational overhead of managing a hyperscale cloud account directly.

Over its history, Fly.io has evolved from an early edge-hosting and WordPress-adjacent project into a general-purpose application and database platform, expanding its region count, introducing the Fly Machines API to support programmatic and ephemeral workloads, launching managed Postgres offerings, and more recently building products aimed at AI agent infrastructure, including tooling for spinning up persistent Linux sandboxes for autonomous coding agents. This progression reflects a broader market shift in which Fly.io has positioned its microVM-based isolation and rapid provisioning as well suited not only to conventional web application hosting but also to the newer demand for isolated, disposable compute environments driven by AI agents and code-execution sandboxes.

paragraphs: Fly.io is a developer-focused public cloud founded in 2017 by Kurt Mackey, Jerome Gravel-Niquet, and Michael Dwan, headquartered in Chicago, Illinois. Rather than hosting an application in one central region, Fly.io runs it as one or more Firecracker microVMs spread across a global network of physical servers, so requests are handled by the data center closest to the requesting user.

The platform has raised roughly 110.5 million dollars in venture funding, including a Series A led by Intel Capital, a Series B led by Andreessen Horowitz, and a 70 million dollar Series C led by EQT Ventures that valued the company at approximately 397 million dollars in 2023. That capital has largely gone toward acquiring and leasing bare-metal server capacity across dozens of cities worldwide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Applications are packaged as container images and deployed as Fly Machines, described in a fly.toml file and managed through the flyctl command-line tool or the Fly Machines API. Traffic is routed with BGP Anycast networking and a custom Rust-based proxy called fly-proxy, which terminates TLS at the nearest data center and, when necessary, backhauls connections to another region over an encrypted WireGuard tunnel.

Beyond compute, Fly.io offers persistent Volumes for durable block storage, managed Fly Postgres for relational databases, a private WireGuard network by default for every organization, and a managed Fly Kubernetes offering, along with integrations for object storage and Redis-compatible caching through partner services.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Fly.io uses fully usage-based pricing: compute is billed per second based on the CPU and RAM preset of each Machine, storage is billed per gigabyte for Volumes and snapshots, and outbound data transfer is billed per gigabyte with rates that vary by region. Dedicated IPv4 addresses, extra SSL certificates, and Fly Kubernetes clusters carry additional flat monthly fees.

features.title: Firecracker microVM compute

features.title: flyctl CLI and fly.toml

features.title: Fly Machines API

features.title: Managed Fly Postgres

features.title: Persistent Volumes
features.description: Durable block storage that attaches to Machines, billed per gigabyte per month, with optional snapshots for backup and recovery.

features.title: Private WireGuard networking
features.description: Every organization gets a private WireGuard-based network by default, letting Machines and developer laptops communicate securely without public exposure.

features.title: Fly Kubernetes

Community reports describe occasional region capacity or networking troubleshooting friction (WireGuard, volume placement) compared with simpler PaaS options

faqs.question: What is Fly.io used for
faqs.answer: Fly.io is used to deploy applications, APIs, and databases so they run physically close to end users in multiple regions, using Firecracker microVMs and a global Anycast network instead of a single centralized server.

faqs.question: Does Fly.io have a free plan
faqs.answer: Fly.io removed its free allowance plans (Hobby, Launch, and Scale) in October 2024. New accounts now get a short trial of about 2 VM hours or 7 days before pay-as-you-go billing with a credit card is required.

faqs.question: How does Fly.io pricing work
faqs.answer: Fly.io bills per second for compute based on the CPU and memory preset of each Machine, per gigabyte for storage Volumes and snapshots, and per gigabyte for outbound data transfer, with additional flat fees for items like dedicated IPv4 addresses and Fly Kubernetes clusters.

faqs.question: Who founded Fly.io and when
faqs.answer: Fly.io was founded in 2017 by Kurt Mackey, Jerome Gravel-Niquet, and Michael Dwan, and is headquartered in Chicago, Illinois.

faqs.question: What technology powers Fly.io
faqs.answer: Fly.io runs customer applications in Firecracker microVMs on physical servers distributed worldwide, using BGP Anycast routing, a Rust-based proxy called fly-proxy, and WireGuard tunnels to route traffic to the nearest available instance.

faqs.question: How does Fly.io compare to Render, Railway, Heroku, and Vercel
faqs.answer: Fly.io emphasizes global multi-region deployment and low-level container control, while Render offers simpler fixed-price single-region hosting, Railway focuses on developer-experience simplicity with usage-based billing, Heroku is a legacy PaaS with a shrinking feature set, and Vercel specializes in frontend frameworks like Next.js rather than general backend and database hosting.

use_cases.title: Globally distributed APIs and backends
use_cases.description: Teams deploy backend services across multiple Fly.io regions so API requests are served from the data center nearest each user, reducing latency for internationally distributed audiences.
use_cases.audience: Backend and platform engineering teams serving a global user base

use_cases.title: Real-time and low-latency applications
use_cases.description: Applications such as multiplayer games, chat, and collaborative tools use Fly.io's Anycast routing and WireGuard backhaul to keep round-trip latency low regardless of user location.
use_cases.audience: Developers building real-time or interactive products

use_cases.title: AI agent sandboxes and ephemeral compute
use_cases.description: The Fly Machines API and fast Firecracker microVM boot times are used to spin up short-lived, isolated Linux environments for AI coding agents and per-user code execution.
use_cases.audience: AI infrastructure teams building agent or sandboxing platforms
